prompt: C- and S-curves dominate the ornament field; no long straight lines in ornamental zones.
Rocaille or shell-scroll motifs are present and structurally integrated into the composition.
Asymmetry is present in motif placement, composition, or figure arrangement.
Ornament density is appropriate: high in frame/edge zones, lower in picture fields.
Palette is soft pastel with gold or ivory accents; no heavy jewel tones dominating.
Light is diffuse and gentle; no cinematic high-contrast chiaroscuro.
Specular highlights are selective (gilding, jewels, satin folds) not broadly metallic.
Overall tonal key is light-to-mid; not predominantly dark.
Floral motifs (roses, peonies, garlands) are present and stylistically coherent.
Putti, cherubs, or mythological figures (if present) match the Rococo playful-sweet register.
Architectural elements (boiserie, mirrors, stucco) show Rococo vocabulary, not Baroque or Neoclassical.
Furniture (if present) shows cabriole legs, bombé shapes, or gilded carved ornament.
Figure proportions evoke Rococo elegance: small head, voluminous skirt/coat, delicate extremities.
Costume includes at least one period marker: panniers, lace, silk ribbons, powdered wig, or tricorn hat.
Figure gestures convey flirtation, leisure, or conversation — not heroic action.
Hair and accessories extend the compositional rhythm and curve language.
Subject is secular, intimate, and playful — fête galante, portrait, boudoir, or salon scene.
Mood reads as elegant, playful, or lightly erotic — not solemn, religious, or austere.
Setting reinforces Rococo world: park, salon, boudoir, garden pavilion, or ornamental interior.
Overall piece reads as Rococo first, not generic vintage, fantasy, or Baroque.
